---
title: "Identification: The J/K Sort and the Winner-Minus-Loser Trade"
date: "2026-07-20"
---

# Identification: The J/K Sort {.title}

::: notes
Welcome back to the reader's guide for Jegadeesh and Titman, part three of eleven. In part two we stated the research question and pinned it to the three-to-twelve-month horizon. Now we get concrete about identification. There is no instrument here and no natural experiment. The identification is the portfolio construction itself, and reading the paper means understanding that construction cold. This part walks through the J-K sort and the headline trade.
:::

## Fix Two Numbers: J and K

- **Formation** (ranking) period: $J$ months
- **Holding** period: $K$ months
- At the end of each month $t$, run the sort
- The whole design hangs on these two choices

::: notes
The construction starts by fixing two numbers. The formation period — also called the ranking period — is J months. The holding period is K months. At the end of each month t, you run a sort based on these two numbers. Everything else in the design follows from this J-and-K choice. J says how far back you look to decide who the winners and losers are. K says how long you hold the resulting positions before you re-sort. The paper studies a grid of J and K values, but the logic of a single cell is what you must internalize first.
:::

## The Three-Step Sort

- Compute each stock's cumulative return over past $J$ months
- Sort all stocks and split into ten **deciles**
- Top decile is winners $W$; bottom decile is losers $L$
- Hold equal-weighted positions for $K$ months, then re-sort

::: notes
Here are the three steps at the end of each month t. Step one: compute each stock's cumulative return over the past J months — that is months t minus J plus one through t. Step two: sort all stocks on that past return and split them into ten deciles. The top decile is the winner portfolio, call it W; the bottom decile is the loser portfolio, call it L. Step three: hold an equal-weighted position in each decile for the next K months, then re-sort. Equal-weighted means each stock in a decile counts the same — not value-weighted by market capitalization. That choice tilts the portfolios toward smaller stocks, which will matter a great deal when we discuss costs.
:::

## The Zero-Cost Winner-Minus-Loser Trade

- Go long winners, short losers, equal dollars
- Legs cancel in cost: net investment is zero
- Return is a pure spread, $r^{W} - r^{L}$
- Positive average spread is the **momentum profit**
- The classic strategy is **six-six**: rank six, hold six

::: notes
The headline trade is the zero-cost winner-minus-loser portfolio. You go long the winners and short the losers in equal dollar amounts. The long and short legs cancel in cost, so the net investment is, notionally, zero, and the return is a pure spread: the winner return minus the loser return. A positive average spread is the momentum profit. The paper reports a grid of these spreads over a J-by-K menu — formation periods of three, six, nine, and twelve months crossed with holding periods of three, six, nine, and twelve months. That is sixteen strategies in one table. The classic, the one everyone quotes, is six-six: rank on six months, hold six months.
:::

## Key Takeaway {.title}

::: callout-tip
## Key takeaway
Identification is the construction itself: sort stocks into deciles on past $J$-month returns, then hold $K$ months. The trade is long winners, short losers — a zero-cost spread, $r^{W}-r^{L}$. The classic is **six-six**. Next: the overlapping trick that makes inference subtle.
:::

::: notes
To summarize part three: with no instrument and no experiment, the identification in this paper is the portfolio construction. You fix a formation length J and a holding length K, sort stocks into ten deciles on their past J-month return, and hold equal-weighted decile positions for K months before re-sorting. The headline trade is the zero-cost winner-minus-loser spread: long the top decile, short the bottom, return equal to winner minus loser. The classic cell is six-six. In part four we examine the clever overlapping-holding-period trick the authors use to squeeze the data — and the serial-correlation problem it quietly creates for your statistics.
:::
